⛷ UX Case Study  ·  Mobile App Prototype

ClearSlope

An Accessible Ski Navigation & Safety App

A mobile application designed to provide real-time ski trail navigation that helps skiers stay on appropriate routes and avoid unintentionally entering more difficult slopes — keeping beginners and intermediates safe without slowing them down.

My Role
Product Design & UX
Platform
iOS Mobile App
Type
Interactive Prototype
Stack
React · TypeScript · Vite
Scroll
Project Brief

Safety through clarity.

"Many beginner and intermediate skiers struggle with traditional trail maps, unclear signage, and complex resort layouts."

ClearSlope addresses this problem by offering step-by-step navigation guidance that actively alerts users when they are approaching trail splits or heading toward slopes beyond their selected difficulty level. The app includes voice notifications, allowing users to receive guidance and warnings without taking out their phone while skiing.

Existing ski apps often focus on performance tracking or static information, but they do not actively protect new skiers from navigation mistakes in real time. ClearSlope fills this gap by prioritizing safety, accessibility, and ease of use during active skiing.

8
Screens designed end-to-end
4
Core feature areas
2
Competitor products analyzed
Primary Design Goal

Reduce the risk of beginner skiers accidentally entering dangerous trails by providing proactive, real-time navigation — not just post-ski stats.

Problem Space

What's actually going wrong on the mountain?

Skiing is inherently disorienting for newcomers. The environment is hostile to phones, attention is split, and mistakes have real physical consequences.

🗺️
Trail maps are cognitively heavy

Traditional paper and PDF trail maps require spatial reasoning, orientation, and significant cognitive load — difficult even at room temperature, nearly impossible while cold, gloved, and moving.

⚠️
Signage fails at decision points

Trail splits happen at speed. Slope signage is often obscured by snow, positioned poorly, or too small to read before the skier has already committed to a direction.

📱
Existing apps are reactive, not protective

Apps like Ski Tracks and Slope record performance after the fact. They tell you what happened — not what's about to happen. No existing app proactively warns users before they enter the wrong trail.

🆘
Emergency information is buried

When a skier is hurt or lost, accessing patrol contact, first aid locations, or emergency numbers requires navigating multiple screens or leaving the app — seconds matter in those moments.

How Might We

How might we help beginner and intermediate skiers navigate complex resort trails confidently — receiving proactive guidance and difficulty-level protection without ever needing to take out their phone?

Competitive Analysis

The market gap ClearSlope fills.

Two leading ski apps were analyzed to understand what already exists — and where the real white space lies.

Product What It Does Well Limitations / Opportunities
Ski Tracks
✓ Strength
Accurate GPS tracking, detailed performance statistics
⚠ Gap
Focuses on post-ski analysis; no real-time navigation or trail protection; high cognitive load for beginners
Slope
✓ Strength
Clean interface; strong run tracking, speed, and vertical drop summaries
⚠ Gap
Designed for reflection rather than guidance; no voice alerts and no very detailed trail maps; does not prevent users from entering more difficult slopes
ClearSlope
(Proposed)
★ Differentiator
Real-time trail navigation, difficulty-based protection, voice guidance while skiing
→ Opportunity
Opportunity to integrate resort-specific live updates and expanded personalization

🔴 Gap #1 — Reactive, not proactive

Neither competitor warns users before they make a navigation mistake. ClearSlope intervenes at the decision point, not after.

🔴 Gap #2 — No voice-first interaction

Both existing apps require looking at the screen. ClearSlope's voice notifications remove the phone-in-hand requirement entirely.

🔴 Gap #3 — Not built for beginners

Performance apps serve intermediate-to-advanced skiers. ClearSlope is explicitly designed for the largest underserved segment: newcomers.

Who We're Designing For

Two primary user personas.

ClearSlope focuses on the skiers most at risk of navigation errors — not the experienced athletes, but the people just finding their footing on the mountain.

🎿
Jamie, 24
First-time skier · College student · Tech-comfortable
Goals
  • Stay on beginner trails and build confidence
  • Not embarrass themselves by going too fast
  • Find the lodge, lifts, and bathrooms easily
Frustrations
  • Resort maps are hard to read with gloves
  • Can't tell where the trail splits lead
  • Worried about accidentally entering a black diamond
🏔️
Sam, 38
Intermediate skier · Parent · Weekend resort visitor
Goals
  • Ski with kids safely at an unfamiliar resort
  • Know which lifts are open before hiking to them
  • Find family-friendly events at the resort
Frustrations
  • Kids lag behind and resort layout is confusing
  • Wasted time skiing to closed lifts
  • Hard to look up emergency info quickly
Interactive Prototype

Try it live.

This is the real, functional prototype — scroll, tap, and navigate through all 8 screens exactly as a user would experience them.

⚙️

Run this once to embed the prototype:

npm run build

Then re-open this file — no server needed.

Click and scroll inside the phone to interact
01
Onboarding
Welcome

Full-bleed mountain imagery establishes brand identity. Two CTAs (Log In / Get Started) reduce decision paralysis immediately.

Design Rationale

Emotional resonance before feature explanation — the image communicates the experience faster than any text could.

02
Auth
Login & Sign Up

Real-time password strength bar, inline field validation, confirm-match check, and SSO placeholders. Errors surface before submission, not after.

Design Rationale

Inline validation respects user time and removes the frustration of a full form reset after hitting "Submit."

03
Core · Dashboard
Home

Personalized greeting, interactive map preview, quick actions, gear carousel (evo.com), upcoming events, and skier reviews in one scrollable view.

Design Rationale

Map card is the hero element — it's the first actionable thing after the greeting, reinforcing the app's core value on every open.

04
Core · Safety
Trail Navigation

Interactive SVG map: pan/zoom gestures, 4 difficulty-rated trails, animated turn-by-turn navigation, voice toggle, real-time cautions, and hazard reporting.

Design Rationale

Voice toggle is persistent and always one tap away — designed for gloved, eyes-off-screen mountain use.

05
Discovery
Events

Horizontal event cards, tab filters (All / Schedule / Reviews), insider ski tips, community photo posts, and CTA buttons.

Design Rationale

Horizontal scroll mirrors familiar mobile patterns (App Store, Netflix) to reduce cognitive friction.

06
Services
Lifts & Transport

Three-tab layout: Lifts (open/closed), Shuttles (countdown timer), Emergency (contacts). Addresses the Sam persona's core pain point directly.

Design Rationale

Emergency is tab 3, not tab 1 — reduces alarm anxiety while keeping it accessible within one tap.

07
Account
Profile

Stats, achievements, inline name editing (no modal), Help Center sub-view, and logout — all within one screen, no nested navigation.

Design Rationale

Achievements create a retention loop — giving users a reason to return even when they're not actively skiing.

Design Rationale

Key decisions and why they matter.

Every product decision was made against a clear user need or safety constraint — not just aesthetic preference.

01

Voice-first navigation as a core feature, not an add-on

Skiing in cold weather with gloves makes screen interaction unreliable. By designing voice notifications as the primary guidance channel — not a secondary option — ClearSlope can be used hands-free. The voice toggle is persistent on the navigation screen rather than buried in settings.

Safety Accessibility
02

Difficulty-based trail protection over free exploration

Rather than showing all trails equally, ClearSlope filters trail recommendations by the user's selected difficulty level. When approaching a harder trail, users receive an active warning. This shifts the app from a passive map to a proactive safety tool — the core differentiator from every existing ski app.

Safety UX
03

Single-screen navigation hub — no nested menus

The bottom navigation bar keeps all four core areas (Home, Events, Transport, Profile) always reachable in one tap. The Trail Navigation screen is intentionally accessed from Home rather than the bottom bar — it is a task, not a destination, which reduces cognitive overhead.

UX Product
04

Teal as the single action color — used sparingly and consistently

Every interactive element, CTA, and active state uses the same brand teal (#00c8b3). No secondary action colors compete with it. This creates an immediate visual grammar: "teal = something you can do" — reducing the need for labels on icon buttons and speeding up task completion time.

Design System Accessibility
05

Gear commerce integrated contextually, not intrusively

Rather than a separate "Shop" tab, gear recommendations appear as a card row within the Home feed — only after the map and quick actions. This respects the user's primary reason for opening the app (navigation) while creating a revenue channel aligned with partner retailers like evo.com. Commerce follows context; it never leads.

Product UX
Design System

Consistent, purposeful, scalable.

A minimal design language built around one brand color, two typefaces, and a cohesive component library.

Color Palette

Brand Teal
#00c8b3 · Primary CTA, active states, icons
Deep Navy
#0f1f2e · CTA backgrounds, dark surfaces
Slate 900
#0f172a · Primary text, headings
Slate 500
#64748b · Secondary text, subtitles
Mint Surface
#eafafa · Page backgrounds, card tints
Alert Red
#ef4444 · Danger, sale tags, errors

Typography

Wordmark — Roboto Slab 800
ClearSlope
Body — Inter 400
Ready for your next skiing adventure?
Caption — Inter 500
Crystal Mountain · Open Today · 4,000 ft vertical

Components

Navigate Save to Calendar Get Started
Staff Picks On Sale Night Event Competition

All components use 12–20px border-radius, 1px borders on light surfaces, and subtle elevation shadows — maintaining iOS-native familiarity for the target audience.

Looking Ahead

From prototype to product.

This prototype validates the UX model and core interaction patterns. Shipping a v1 would require these investments, prioritized by user impact.

P0 · Safety
01

Real-Time Hazard & Difficulty Alerts

Connect GPS location to trail boundary data and fire proactive voice alerts when approaching a difficulty escalation — the core product promise.

P0 · Core
02

Native Mobile (GPS + Background Audio)

Port to React Native / Expo for true GPS trail tracking, offline map caching, haptic feedback, and native TTS for background voice navigation.

P1 · Data
03

Live Resort Data Integration

Connect real lift status, snow conditions, and shuttle ETAs via resort APIs. Push notifications for closures and new powder conditions.

P1 · Research
04

On-Mountain Usability Testing

Validate the navigation UX under real conditions — cold, gloves, time pressure. Test with target personas (Jamie, Sam) at an actual resort.

P2 · Growth
05

Multi-Resort Platform

Abstract the resort data model so any ski resort can be onboarded — enabling ClearSlope to become a platform product rather than a single-resort app.

P2 · Retention
06

Social & Achievement System

Expand achievements with earned badges, a friends leaderboard, trail completion logging, and shareable post-run summaries to drive DAU and retention.

ClearSlope

UX Case Study · Mobile App Prototype · 2026

React · TypeScript · Vite · Tailwind CSS
